votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the primality check and the conditional logic implied by the Coq proposition.
The Python code correctly implements the primality check and accurately reflects the conditional logic of the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking primality and returning whether 'res' matches 'x' or 'y' accordingly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. In Coq, x_or_y_spec is a conjunction of two implications: (prime n -> res = x) and (~prime n -> res = y). For any concrete n, exactly one of 'prime n' or '~prime n' holds. When prime n holds, the first implication requires res = x (the second is vacuously true). When ~prime n holds, the second implication requires res = y (the first is vacuously true). The Python code checks primality and returns whether res equals the appropriate value (x if prime, y if not prime), which matches the Coq spec's concrete behavior for all inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. In Coq, x_or_y_spec is a conjunction of two implications: (prime n -> res = x) and (~prime n -> res = y). For any concrete n, exactly one of 'prime n' or '~prime n' holds. When prime n holds, the first implication requires res = x (second is vacuously true). When ~prime n holds, the second implication requires res = y (first is vacuously true). The Python code checks primality and returns whether res equals x (if prime) or y (if not prime), which matches this behavior for all concrete inputs. The is_prime helper correctly implements primality checking consistent with Coq's prime definition.

Python’s is_prime matches Coq’s Z prime semantics (only integers >1 with no divisors are prime), and x_or_y_spec returns res==x if prime else res==y, which is exactly the concrete behavior required by the Coq spec’s two implications.
Python returns res == x when n is prime and res == y otherwise, matching the Coq spec’s two implications; the primality check aligns with Coq’s notion (primes are > 1).

The Python function returns True exactly when res equals x if n is prime and equals y otherwise, matching the Coq spec’s conjunction of implications. The primality test treats n <= 1 and negatives as non-prime and aligns with Znumtheory.prime for Z.
